Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm 28 and working while taking seven online courses, so I had to squeeze studying into whatever moments I could find. I'd usually study late at night after work or early in the morning before my shift. I tried to dedicate about 20 hours a week, but it was scattered across random pockets of time rather than any organized schedule. I mostly relied on reading and writing things out since that's how I learn best, so I'd print materials when I could afford to and annotate them heavily.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and what was happening at work. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because I was exhausted from double shifts, then I'd have to cram everything into a few days before assignments were due. I wanted to be more consistent, but life just didn't allow for that luxury. The anxiety of knowing I was falling behind was constant, but I couldn't always do anything about it in the moment.
Q3: Usually panic about a deadline would prompt me to log in. I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or realize I hadn't checked announcements in a while and worry I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to ease my anxiety, to see what was coming up so I could mentally prepare myself. It wasn't a calm, routine check-in—it was more like putting out fires constantly.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. With seven courses and everything else going on, the LMS never became a comfortable routine for me. There were brief periods where I'd try to check in every morning, but that habit would break as soon as work got demanding or I had a bad anxiety day. The closest I got to routine was downloading everything I needed at once so I wouldn't have to log in as often.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were most useful to me because I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and annotate them on paper or in a basic text editor. The assignment rubrics were also crucial—I'd study those carefully to make sure I understood exactly what was expected since I couldn't afford to waste time on things that wouldn't count toward my grade.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—I only posted twice the whole course. I felt like I didn't have time to engage in conversations when I was barely keeping up with the required work. The videos I watched some of, but honestly, watching over 100 minutes of video felt like a luxury I couldn't afford when I could read the same content faster. I'd skim videos at most, looking for specific information.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to my grade or helped me understand something I was struggling with. I had to be ruthlessly practical because my time and energy were so limited. If something seemed like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to the assessments, I'd skip it. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped because they broke things into smaller, more manageable pieces that felt achievable.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a cruel joke sometimes. Like, I could barely complete the required work—how was I supposed to find time for extras? I'd look at them and feel guilty for not doing them, which added to my anxiety. Required activities at least had clear consequences, so I could prioritize them. Optional ones just made me feel like I was failing even more than I already was.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and print them when I could, or save them to my phone to read during breaks at work. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes and summaries because writing helps me remember. I also used free resources I found online—YouTube explanations, Wikipedia for background concepts—none of which would show up in the LMS at all.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours with my printed notes and notebooks, working through concepts, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd re-read my handwritten summaries on the bus to work. Sometimes I'd study with a coworker who was also in school, quizzing each other during slow periods. All that effort was invisible to the system but very real to me.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a PDF or watch a video once to get the gist, then work with that material offline for days or weeks. With limited internet time and the cost of data, I couldn't afford to keep streaming or re-accessing things online. I'd save everything I could and work from those saved files. My offline study time far exceeded my online time.
Q12: There were several points where I nearly gave up completely. When my anxiety was at its worst, I couldn't focus on anything—I'd just stare at the screen feeling overwhelmed. Work stress compounded everything; there were weeks where I was scheduled for extra shifts and had no energy left for school. The sadness would creep in and make even logging in feel like climbing a mountain.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. The workload of seven courses was honestly too much, and I was always confused about which course needed attention first. Fatigue from work made it hard to concentrate even when I had time. My responsibilities at home and financially meant school often had to take a backseat. The LMS became something I dreaded rather than a helpful tool.
Q14: Many times. I'd log in and click through materials without really absorbing anything because my mind was elsewhere—worrying about bills, exhausted from work, or just too anxious to focus. I'd have the readings open but realize I'd read the same paragraph five times without understanding it. My eyes were on the screen, but my brain wasn't engaged at all.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew it would hurt me later. Sometimes the anxiety was so overwhelming that I'd avoid the LMS entirely because seeing how much I had to do made me feel worse. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel just as bad. It was a vicious cycle of avoidance and guilt that made everything harder.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd scramble in the last few days before something was due, which is why my attendance was so low but my assignment completion was decent. The deadline pressure would override my anxiety temporarily and force me into action. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could function given everything else going on.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did motivate me to engage more—especially positive feedback that showed my effort was paying off. But feedback often came too late to help with the next assignment, or I was too overwhelmed to process it properly. The gamified task elements gave me more immediate feedback, which actually helped because I could see small wins along the way.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming with seven courses running simultaneously. I struggled to keep track of which deadlines belonged to which course. When things were clearly organized with obvious due dates and straightforward requirements, I could manage better. But when the structure was confusing or spread across multiple pages, I'd waste precious time just figuring out what I needed to do.
Q19: Clear, downloadable materials made engagement easier because I could work offline. The gamified elements they introduced helped break the overwhelming workload into smaller pieces. What made it harder was when important information was buried in discussion threads I didn't have time to read, or when assignments required constant online access I couldn't always maintain. The sheer number of courses made everything harder.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—desperately trying to keep up, clicking through everything to understand what's required, but running out of time or energy to actually complete the work. High activity might show someone who's struggling and searching for help, not someone who's successfully learning. It could also be anxiety-driven clicking, trying to feel in control without actually accomplishing anything.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My exam score was okay and I passed, but my LMS activity was pretty low because I did so much work offline. I'd download materials once and study them for weeks without logging back in. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying, but I was working incredibly hard—just not in ways the system could see.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logs in and what they click on, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if someone is staring blankly at a screen or deeply focused. They miss the handwritten notes, the discussions with classmates outside the platform, the mental processing that happens while commuting or working. They show a shadow of learning, not the real thing.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in genuine effort to learn, even when it's hard. It's not just clicking through screens—it's wrestling with concepts until they make sense, connecting ideas to your life, and persisting despite obstacles. For me, engagement often looked like exhausted determination, studying on my breaks at work, refusing to give up even when everything felt impossible.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to stay in school while working and dealing with anxiety. It can't capture the tears of frustration or the pride when I finally understood something difficult. It doesn't know about the sacrifices I made—skipping meals to afford printing, staying up until 2am after a full shift. It sees my low attendance and few discussion posts, but it doesn't see my heart in this, my desperate hope that education will change my circumstances.
